findings.title: captureSnapshots uses local-TZ midnight for `date`, causing duplicate/wrong-day snapshots in prod (UTC)
findings.description: The snapshot scheduler calls `new Date()` then `.setHours(0,0,0,0)`, which produces the *local-time* midnight. The prod server runs UTC, but the `date` column in `ProjectSnapshot` is typed as `@db.Date` and Prisma persists it using UTC date components. When the API process runs on a server whose TZ is anything other than UTC (e.g. Europe/Paris +01/+02), `startOfDay` is shifted: on a Paris server, midnight local = 23:00 the previous UTC day, so the snapshot lands on the wrong calendar date. The same value is used for the de-duplication query — if the stored row was created yesterday (UTC) but today (Paris), the de-dup misses it and a second snapshot is inserted, breaking the @@unique([projectId, date]) invariant or silently writing a duplicate via `skipDuplicates`.
findings.root_cause: `setHours(0,0,0,0)` anchors midnight on the process local timezone rather than UTC, inconsistent with the `@db.Date` column semantics that Prisma resolves from UTC date components.
findings.impact: In production (UTC, no drift), this is a no-op. On a non-UTC server (e.g. the Europe/Paris VPS), every snapshot is off by one calendar day; the de-duplication query also fails because the stored UTC date and the query date disagree, so the cron can insert a second snapshot for the same project on the same Paris day.
findings.suggested_fix: Replace `setHours(0,0,0,0)` with `new Date(Date.UTC(now.getUTCFullYear(), now.getUTCMonth(), now.getUTCDate()))` to pin midnight to UTC, consistent with how `holidays.service.ts` and the leave year-window helpers construct their date boundaries.
acceptance_criteria: On a Europe/Paris host, `captureSnapshots` inserts a snapshot with `date = UTC midnight of the current Paris calendar day`
The de-duplication query finds the previously-inserted row on the same Paris calendar day and skips re-insertion
Unit test: mock `new Date()` to 2026-01-01T23:30:00Z (= Jan 2 in Europe/Paris), assert `startOfDay` equals 2026-01-01T00:00:00Z
findings.notes: The leave-year-window helpers (parisYearWindow, and so on) consistently use Date.UTC / fromZonedTime. This one service is the outlier.

findings.title: UsersService.update: userService delete+createMany runs outside the user.update transaction, leaving service memberships temporarily inconsistent
findings.description: The service-membership sync (deleteMany + createMany) executes before the `user.update`. These three operations are issued as three separate database statements with no wrapping `$transaction`. If `createMany` or `user.update` fails after `deleteMany` has committed, the user's service memberships are wiped and never restored. A concurrent read between `deleteMany` and `createMany` also observes a user with no service memberships — which can yield wrong RBAC perimeter calculations (leave approvals, task scoping) for the duration of the gap.
findings.root_cause: No `$transaction` wraps the three sequential Prisma calls that form a single logical mutation.
findings.impact: A failure between `deleteMany` and `createMany` leaves the user with zero service memberships, silently breaking all perimeter-based access-control checks until an admin manually repairs the record. A concurrent read during the gap returns an incorrect (empty) membership set.
findings.suggested_fix: Wrap the entire service-sync + user.update block inside `this.prisma.$transaction(async (tx) => { ... })`, using `tx.userService.deleteMany`, `tx.userService.createMany`, and `tx.user.update`. This matches the pattern already used in `users.service.ts` line 948 for the full-erasure path.
acceptance_criteria: All three DB mutations (deleteMany, createMany, user.update) are inside a single $transaction
If user.update throws (e.g. unique constraint on login), the deleteMany is rolled back and the original service memberships are preserved
Integration test: inject a failing user.update and assert the user's service memberships remain unchanged after the error

findings.title: resetPassword: user.update and token.usedAt mark are non-atomic — token can be double-consumed under concurrent requests
findings.description: The password update and the token invalidation (`usedAt = now()`) are two sequential, non-transactional DB writes. Between the two writes, a second concurrent call with the same token passes the `usedAt !== null` check (line 555: token was found, `usedAt` is still null) and proceeds to hash and apply a new password, setting the user's password to the second caller's value. The first caller's password is then overwritten and the intended password reset fails. This is a classic double-submit / replay window on the reset token.
findings.root_cause: No `$transaction` wraps `user.update` and `passwordResetToken.update`, leaving a window where the token appears unconsumed to a concurrent reader.
findings.impact: An attacker who intercepts or guesses a valid reset token and replays it before it is marked used can overwrite the victim's password to their own value, achieving full account takeover.
findings.suggested_fix: Wrap both writes in a single `$transaction`. Additionally, consider changing the token lookup to a conditional update (`updateMany({ where: { token: tokenHash, usedAt: null } })`) and checking the affected count to detect concurrent consumption atomically.
acceptance_criteria: user.update and passwordResetToken.update (usedAt) execute inside a single $transaction
Concurrent simultaneous calls with the same token result in exactly one password update; the second call receives an error
Test: two concurrent resetPassword calls with the same token — only one succeeds
findings.notes: The generateResetToken method correctly invalidates prior tokens atomically. This resetPassword path does not apply the same discipline.

findings.title: addDependency allows two orphan tasks (projectId = null) to be linked, violating the 'same project' invariant
findings.description: The cross-project guard uses a strict JavaScript inequality (`!==`). When both `task.projectId` and `dependsOnTask.projectId` are `null`, the comparison evaluates `null !== null` which is `false`, so the guard passes silently. This means any two orphan tasks (meetings, cross-cutting tasks) can be linked as dependencies regardless of whether they have any business relationship. The orphan-task feature is intentional per CLAUDE.md, but linking arbitrary orphan tasks as dependencies creates invisible coupling that persists even when one of the tasks is later attached to a project via `attachToProject`.
findings.root_cause: The `!==` operator does not distinguish `null !== null` (both orphans) from `null !== 'project-uuid'` (different projects), treating both cases identically while only one should be an error.
findings.impact: Any user can link any two orphan tasks as dependencies, potentially creating circular-dependency chains or polluting the dependency graph with semantically unrelated tasks. If a linked orphan is later attached to a project, the cross-project dependency remains, bypassing the same-project invariant for that pair.
findings.suggested_fix: Add an explicit null check: `if (task.projectId === null || task.projectId !== dependsOnTask.projectId)`. Orphan tasks should not participate in dependency relationships (they have no project scope to define what 'same scope' means), or the check should be `task.projectId !== null && task.projectId === dependsOnTask.projectId` (i.e. same non-null project).
acceptance_criteria: addDependency(orphanTask1, orphanTask2) throws BadRequestException
addDependency(projectTask, orphanTask) throws BadRequestException
addDependency(projectTask1, projectTask2_same_project) succeeds as before

findings.title: PersonalTodosService.create: count-then-create is non-atomic — concurrent requests can push todo count above 20
findings.description: The 20-item hard cap is enforced by a `count` query followed by a `create` outside any transaction. Two concurrent requests by the same user both read `count = 19`, both pass the `>= 20` check, and both proceed to `create`, resulting in 21 items. CLAUDE.md documents this as a hard invariant ('Personal Todos: hard-coded 20-item limit'). The race is realistic via rapid UI interaction or API tooling.
findings.root_cause: The count-check and the create are two separate, unprotected DB operations with no locking or atomic guard.
findings.impact: The 20-item invariant documented in CLAUDE.md can be violated, resulting in more than 20 todos per user. Downstream code (UI rendering, any report that assumes the cap) may behave incorrectly.
findings.suggested_fix: Wrap the count + create in a `$transaction` and re-check the count inside the transaction, or use a DB-level constraint (`CHECK (count(*) <= 20)` via a trigger, or an application-level unique index with a sequence). The simplest correct fix is: `await this.prisma.$transaction(async (tx) => { const count = await tx.personalTodo.count(...); if (count >= MAX_TODOS) throw ...; return tx.personalTodo.create(...); })`.
acceptance_criteria: Two concurrent create requests from the same user at count=19 result in exactly one success and one 400 error
After concurrent requests, the DB row count for that user is exactly 20
Existing sequential behaviour (creating up to 20 succeeds, the 21st fails) is preserved
findings.notes: The auto-cleanup in findByUser runs before create is called, reducing the window slightly, but it does not close the race.

findings.title: getLeaveBalance uses server-local new Date().getFullYear() — on a Europe/Paris server the 'current year' is wrong between Dec 31 23:00 and Jan 1 00:00 UTC
findings.description: All leave accounting is anchored on Europe/Paris (`LEAVE_TIMEZONE = 'Europe/Paris'`). The `parisYearWindow` helper correctly converts Paris year boundaries to UTC. However, `getLeaveBalance` derives `currentYear` from `new Date().getFullYear()`, which uses the *process local timezone* rather than Paris time. On a UTC server this is a no-op. On a Europe/Paris server it would appear correct. But the real edge case: on Jan 1, between 00:00 and 01:00 Paris time (= Dec 31 23:00–Jan 1 00:00 UTC), `new Date().getFullYear()` on a UTC server returns the *old* year (e.g. 2025) while Paris employees are already in the new year (2026). Their balance is computed against 2025's allocations and 2025 leaves, showing the wrong year's balance.
findings.root_cause: `new Date().getFullYear()` does not account for the Europe/Paris timezone when determining which calendar year is 'current' for French public-sector employees.
findings.impact: For employees using the leave portal between 23:00 and 00:00 UTC on New Year's Eve (= 00:00–01:00 Jan 1 Paris), `getLeaveBalance` returns 2025's balance instead of 2026's, showing a potentially stale or confusing balance during a narrow but deterministic window.
findings.suggested_fix: Replace `new Date().getFullYear()` with `Number(formatInTimeZone(new Date(), 'Europe/Paris', 'yyyy'))` using the already-imported `date-fns-tz` helpers, consistent with how `parisDayKey` and `parisYearWindow` derive the Paris calendar context.
acceptance_criteria: At 2026-12-31T23:30:00Z (= 2027-01-01 00:30 Paris), getLeaveBalance returns year=2027
At 2026-01-01T00:30:00Z (= 2026-01-01 01:30 Paris), getLeaveBalance returns year=2026
Unit test: mock Date.now() to Dec 31 23:30 UTC and assert currentYear=next year
findings.notes: The impact is a 1-hour window per year. Low frequency, but deterministic and incorrectly computes a financial variable (leave balance).

findings.title: importLeaves bulk import: getHolidayKeySet is called inside the $transaction — Prisma nested reads on the outer prisma client inside a tx client context may bypass the tx isolation
findings.description: `importLeaves` wraps its loop in `this.prisma.$transaction(async (tx) => { ... })`. Inside the transaction body, the code calls `this.getHolidayKeySet()`, which internally calls `this.holidaysService.findByRange()` — a method that uses `this.prisma` (the root Prisma client, not `tx`). This is the same pattern the code intentionally avoids in `create()` (commented 'Référentiel statique, lu hors transaction'), but here it is inside the transaction. Using the root prisma client inside a transaction body means the holiday read is on a different connection and is not subject to the transaction's isolation level. This is architectural inconsistency but not a correctness bug in practice (holidays are reference data), EXCEPT that it means the holiday read does NOT hold the `tx` connection — creating N extra connections per imported leave row inside the transaction, risking connection pool exhaustion for large CSV imports.
findings.root_cause: `getHolidayKeySet` uses `this.prisma` unconditionally and cannot be passed a transaction client; its caller inside the tx body therefore establishes a separate connection per invocation.
findings.impact: For large CSV imports, each row opens an additional DB connection for the holiday read inside the outer transaction. This risks Prisma connection pool exhaustion (pool default: 5–10 connections) or significant performance degradation. The import is not technically wrong (holidays are read-only reference data), but the repeated per-row query inside the transaction is wasteful.
findings.suggested_fix: Pre-fetch the holiday key set once BEFORE the transaction (as done in create()/update()), computing the union of all rows' date ranges, and pass the result into the loop. This removes the per-row nested read and is consistent with the documented pattern.
acceptance_criteria: getHolidayKeySet is called at most once per importLeaves invocation, before the $transaction block
The resulting holidayKeys set covers the union of all rows' [startDate, endDate] ranges
A 100-row CSV import triggers exactly 1 holiday lookup, not 100

findings.title: tasks.update: assigning an epicId or milestoneId that belongs to a different project than the task's (possibly updated) projectId is not validated
findings.description: The `create` method (lines 153–182) correctly validates that `epic.projectId === projectId` and `milestone.projectId === projectId`. The `update` method validates only that the epic/milestone *exist* but does NOT check that they belong to the same project as the task (either the task's existing `projectId` or the newly supplied `projectId` in the DTO). An attacker or UI bug can therefore link a task in project A to an epic from project B, violating the referential integrity that the create path enforces. The DB schema may not carry a composite FK for this cross-entity constraint.
findings.root_cause: The update validation was written as a subset of create validation, omitting the cross-project membership check for epicId and milestoneId.
findings.impact: A task can be in project A while referencing an epic or milestone from project B. This corrupts project-level reporting (epic progress, milestone completion counts) and may cause 'Projet introuvable' or incorrect scope responses in the project detail endpoint.
findings.suggested_fix: In the update method's epicId validation block, after confirming the epic exists, add: `const effectiveProjectId = projectId ?? existingTask.projectId; if (epic.projectId !== effectiveProjectId) throw new BadRequestException("L'epic n'appartient pas au même projet");`. Apply the same pattern for milestoneId.
acceptance_criteria: PATCH /tasks/:id with epicId belonging to a different project returns 400
PATCH /tasks/:id with milestoneId belonging to a different project returns 400
PATCH /tasks/:id with epicId null (clearing the epic) still succeeds
Existing valid updates (epic in the same project) are unaffected

findings.title: validateImport (tasks): end <= start is classified as a warning, but create() rejects end < start as an error — inconsistent boundary semantics
findings.description: The `validateImport` dry-run marks equal-start-and-end-date tasks as `warning` (not `error`). The `create()` method (line 210) explicitly allows equal dates: `if (end < start)` (strict less-than). The validation preview therefore incorrectly flags valid single-day tasks (`start == end`) as warnings, confusing operators. The preview message 'antérieure ou égale' is also misleading since the actual constraint only rejects `end < start`.
findings.root_cause: The validation preview uses `<=` while the runtime constraint uses `<`.
findings.impact: Single-day tasks in the CSV are shown as warnings in the preview but actually import successfully, causing operator confusion and potentially causing valid imports to be abandoned.
findings.suggested_fix: Change line 1673 to `if (end < start)` to match the runtime constraint and update the message to 'La date de fin est antérieure à la date de début'.
acceptance_criteria: validateImport: a task with startDate == endDate is classified as 'valid', not 'warning'
validateImport: a task with endDate < startDate is classified as 'error'

findings.title: canValidate: active delegation check is scope-agnostic — any active delegate can validate any leave, regardless of whether the delegator manages the leave's owner
findings.description: The delegation fallback in `canValidate` returns `true` for any active delegation where `delegateId === validatorId`, regardless of which `delegatorId` granted it. A delegate appointed by manager A (who manages service X) is thus authorized to approve leave requests for employees in service Y, even if the delegator has no authority over service Y. The earlier checks (assigned validatorId, APPROVE_LEAVES + service scope) correctly implement scope. The delegation fallback discards scope entirely.
findings.root_cause: The delegation lookup omits a join back to the delegator's authority scope.
findings.impact: A delegate granted by any manager effectively gains global leave approval authority for the duration of the delegation, regardless of the delegating manager's perimeter. This violates the 'RESPONSABLE scope périmètre' invariant noted in MEMORY.md.
findings.suggested_fix: Extend the delegation query to also check that the delegator (the `delegatorId`) would have authority over the leave's owner. Specifically: for each active delegation where `delegateId = validatorId`, check that `canManageLeave(leave.userId, delegation.delegatorId, delegatorRole)` would be true — meaning the delegator is in the leave owner's perimeter.
acceptance_criteria: A delegate from manager A (service X only) cannot approve leave of an employee in service Y
A delegate from an ADMIN-role manager (leaves:manage_any) can approve any leave
Existing behaviour for correctly-scoped delegations is preserved
findings.notes: The MANAGE_DELEGATIONS permission gate on createDelegation limits who can create delegations, partially mitigating this. But anyone with that permission (MANAGER+ via template) can create a delegation that grants globally-scoped approval power to the delegate.

verified_clean: tasks.service.ts checkCircularDependency: BFS correctly uses a visited set to prevent infinite loops on cycles
leaves.service.ts create(): Serializable transaction with one-shot P2034 retry correctly prevents balance TOCTOU on the create path
leaves.service.ts approve()/reject()/cancel()/requestCancel()/rejectCancellation(): all status transitions wrap a re-read inside $transaction (TOCTOU guard for concurrent transitions)
auth/refresh-token.service.ts rotate(): Serializable transaction wraps find+revoke+issue; reuse detection revokes all tokens on replay
audit-persistence.service.ts log(): advisory lock pg_advisory_xact_lock serialises all audit inserts; rowHash chain is recomputable from stored fields
leave-year-window.ts calculateLeaveDays / splitLeaveByYear: half-day arithmetic is Paris-anchored via parisDayKey; holiday exclusion is additive and optional
projects.service.ts update(): $transaction wraps projectClient sync when clientIds is supplied (COR-018 fix is present)
projects.service.ts archive()/unarchive(): $transaction correctly wraps project mutation + audit write (DAT-006 pattern)
projects.service.ts hardDelete(): audit log emitted before delete; dependency pre-check prevents P2003 from leaking
auth.service.ts resetPassword: password hash verification after bcrypt.compare (usedAt check), and session invalidation (refresh + nbf bump) are all present
time-tracking.service.ts ensureDailyCapNotExceeded(): daily cap check uses UTC date components (Date.UTC), not local-time
holidays.service.ts importFrenchHolidays(): All fixed holidays use Date.UTC constructor; calculateEaster returns Date.UTC; addDays uses setUTCDate
